title: Complete Binary Tree Inserter
description:
A complete binary tree is a binary tree in which every level, except possibly the last, is completely filled, and all nodes are as far left as possible.

Design an algorithm to insert a new node to a complete binary tree keeping it complete after the insertion.

Implement the CBTInserter class:

*   CBTInserter(TreeNode root) Initializes the data structure with the root of the complete binary tree.
*   int insert(int v) Inserts a TreeNode into the tree with value Node.val == val so that the tree remains complete, and returns the value of the parent of the inserted TreeNode.
*   TreeNode get_root() Returns the root node of the tree.

solutionCode.python:
```python
def projectionArea(grid):
    n = len(grid)
    top, front, side = 0, 0, 0
    for i in range(n):
        max_front, max_side = 0, 0
        for j in range(n):
            if grid[i][j] > 0:
                top += 1
            max_front = max(max_front, grid[i][j])
            max_side = max(max_side, grid[j][i])
        front += max_front
        side += max_side
    return top + front + side
```
    
    We need to find the area of three projections: top, front, and side. We initialize three variables to store the area of each projection. For the top projection, we increment the area by 1 if there is a cube in the cell. For the front and side projections, we find the maximum height of cubes along each row and column respectively, and sum them up.

We iterate through the grid, and for each row i, we initialize two variables maxFront and maxSide to keep track of the maximum height of cubes in row i and column i respectively. For each column j, we check if there is a cube at grid[i][j] and increment the top area if true. We update maxFront and maxSide with the maximum values of grid[i][j] and grid[j][i] respectively. After iterating through all columns in the row, we add maxFront and maxSide to the front and side areas respectively.

After iterating through all the rows, we return the sum of the areas of the top, front, and side projections.
